hook: You already know that $\mathbb{R}^n$ is a vector space where vectors are lists of numbers you can add and scale. But what if your 'vectors' were polynomials like $3x^2 - 2x + 1$? You can certainly add two polynomials and multiply one by a scalar — but does that make the set of all polynomials a genuine vector space? The question is: which of the ten vector space axioms actually need to be verified, and how do you check them for objects that look nothing like arrows or coordinate tuples?
concept: A vector space is any set equipped with addition and scalar multiplication satisfying ten axioms — closure, commutativity, associativity, identity, inverses, and distributive laws. The set $\mathcal{P}_n$ of all polynomials of degree at most $n$ (with real coefficients) forms a vector space under the usual polynomial addition and scalar multiplication, because every one of the ten axioms holds for those operations.
anatomy: The key structural parts of $\mathcal{P}_n$ as a vector space are: the underlying set $\mathcal{P}_n = \{a_0 + a_1 x + \cdots + a_n x^n \mid a_i \in \mathbb{R}\}$; the addition operation $(p + q)(x) = p(x) + q(x)$, which adds corresponding coefficients; the scalar multiplication operation $(cp)(x) = c \cdot p(x)$, which scales every coefficient by $c \in \mathbb{R}$; the zero vector, which is the zero polynomial $\mathbf{0}(x) = 0$ (all coefficients zero); and the additive inverse $-p$, which negates every coefficient of $p$.

worked_example.description_latex: We start by identifying two specific elements of $\mathcal{P}_2$ to use as concrete test cases. Naming $p$ and $q$ explicitly means every axiom check will be grounded in numbers rather than abstract symbols, which makes it much easier to see what is really going on.

We verify closure under addition by computing $p + q$ and checking whether the result is still in $\mathcal{P}_2$. The result $x^2 + 2x - 3$ has degree at most 2, so yes — $\mathcal{P}_2$ is closed under addition, meaning adding two elements never takes you outside the set.

We verify closure under scalar multiplication by scaling $p$ by the real number $3$. Every coefficient is multiplied by $3$, and the degree stays at most 2, so $3p \in \mathcal{P}_2$ — the set does not 'escape' when we scale a polynomial.

We identify the zero vector of this space. It is the polynomial whose every coefficient is zero, and $p(x) + \mathbf{0}(x) = p(x)$ for any $p$ — this is the additive identity axiom, and it is satisfied because the zero polynomial clearly belongs to $\mathcal{P}_2$.

Finally, we confirm the additive inverse axiom: for any $p$, the polynomial $-p$ obtained by negating every coefficient also lies in $\mathcal{P}_2$ and satisfies $p + (-p) = \mathbf{0}$. Together with the other checks, this shows $\mathcal{P}_2$ satisfies all the essential structural requirements of a vector space.

partial_example.description_latex: We are working in $\mathcal{P}_3$ and we want to verify both closure axioms. Let us first identify $p$ and $q$ as our two polynomials — notice both have degree at most 3, so they genuinely belong to $\mathcal{P}_3$.
Compute $p(x) + q(x)$ by adding corresponding coefficients for $x^3$, $x^2$, $x^1$, and the constant term. Then state whether or not the result belongs to $\mathcal{P}_3$ and why.

For closure under scalar multiplication we scale $p$ by $-2$. Each coefficient is multiplied by $-2$, and the degree remains 3, so $-2p \in \mathcal{P}_3$ — this confirms the closure axiom holds for this scalar.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Identify the zero vector and the additive inverse } -p(x) \text{ in } \mathcal{P}_3.
partial_example.description_latex: Write down the zero vector of $\mathcal{P}_3$ explicitly, then write down $-p(x)$ and verify that $p(x) + (-p(x)) = \mathbf{0}$. This step tests whether you understand what the zero vector and additive inverse look like for polynomial spaces specifically.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Let $p(x) = 4x - 7$ and $q(x) = -4x + 2$ be elements of $\mathcal{P}_1$. Compute $p(x) + q(x)$ and state whether the result is in $\mathcal{P}_1$.
practice_problems.answer_latex: (p+q)(x) = -5, \text{ which is in } \mathcal{P}_1 \text{ (a degree-0 polynomial, i.e., a constant)}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Verify the closure under scalar multiplication axiom for $p(x) = 3x^2 - x + 2 \in \mathcal{P}_2$ and scalar $c = -5$.
practice_problems.answer_latex: (-5p)(x) = -15x^2 + 5x - 10, \text{ which has degree } \leq 2, \text{ so } -5p \in \mathcal{P}_2. \text{ Closure holds.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: What is the zero vector of $\mathcal{P}_4$? Write it in standard polynomial form and identify all its coefficients.
Consider $\mathcal{P}_2$ and the subset $S = \{p \in \mathcal{P}_2 \mid p(1) = 0\}$. Show that $S$ is closed under addition and scalar multiplication, i.e., that $S$ is a subspace of $\mathcal{P}_2$.
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{If } p(1)=0 \text{ and } q(1)=0, \text{ then } (p+q)(1)=0+0=0 \text{ and } (cp)(1)=c\cdot 0=0, \text{ so } S \text{ is closed under both operations. Also } \mathbf{0}(1)=0, \text{ so } S \text{ is a subspace.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A student claims that the set $\mathcal{P}$ of ALL polynomials (no degree restriction) is also a vector space under the usual addition and scalar multiplication. Identify whether the ten axioms still hold, focusing on whether $\mathcal{P}$ is closed under its operations.
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Yes, } \mathcal{P} \text{ is a vector space: adding any two polynomials or scaling one always yields a polynomial (still finite degree), so all closure and other axioms hold. The zero polynomial } \mathbf{0} \in \mathcal{P} \text{, and every } p \text{ has an inverse } -p \in \mathcal{P}.

common_mistakes: Students assume that because the degree of $p + q$ could be less than $n$ (e.g., leading terms cancel), the sum is no longer in $\mathcal{P}_n$ — but $\mathcal{P}_n$ contains ALL polynomials of degree AT MOST $n$, including constants and the zero polynomial, so a drop in degree never breaks closure.

Students treat the zero vector of $\mathcal{P}_n$ as the number $0$ rather than the zero polynomial function; this matters when checking the additive identity axiom, because the zero vector must be an element of the same space — a polynomial — not a scalar from $\mathbb{R}$.

When checking whether a subset of $\mathcal{P}_n$ is a subspace, students verify closure under addition but forget to check that the zero polynomial belongs to the subset, which is a required condition; always confirm the zero vector is in the candidate subspace before claiming it is one.
untested_variants: Finding a basis and the dimension of $\mathcal{P}_n$ (e.g., showing $\{1, x, x^2, \ldots, x^n\}$ is a basis), which connects the vector space structure to coordinate representations.
Determining whether a given set of polynomials (e.g., $\{x^2+1,\, x-1,\, x^2+x\}$) is linearly independent or spans $\mathcal{P}_2$.
Working with $\mathcal{P}_n$ over the complex numbers $\mathbb{C}$ rather than $\mathbb{R}$, where scalar multiplication uses complex scalars.
Verifying all ten vector space axioms in full formal detail for $\mathcal{P}_n$, rather than focusing only on the closure, zero vector, and additive inverse axioms as done in this lesson.
